title: Chapter 5: Gaslight Season
content: He remembered the tea.

I never told him about the tea. I know I didn't because I'd been keeping a list in my head — small things I hadn't shared yet, breadcrumbs I could leave later, conversations I could look forward to. Lapsang souchong. Three steepings, no milk. I'd been saving it.
And yet there it was, Tuesday morning, a thermos of it waiting on the shop counter with a note in his handwriting: *For the woman who takes her time.*
"How did you—" I started.
"You mentioned it," he said. That smile. That calm, certain smile. "Weeks ago. Don't you remember?"

I didn't. But I said nothing because it felt like a small thing to be wrong about. Because the tea was perfect — smoked and dark and exactly right. Because it was easier to believe I'd forgotten than to believe something stranger.
That was the first crack. I didn't hear it.

The book came Wednesday. A first edition of *The Bloody Chamber*. Blue cloth binding, gold lettering faded to a whisper. I'd never told anyone I wanted it. I'd never even said it aloud — it was a private wanting, the kind you keep in a locked room inside yourself because speaking it would make it impossible. Some wishes only come true if you never confess them.

"I found it in an estate sale across the vale," Julian said, holding it out like it was nothing. Like first editions of Angela Carter appeared in his hands the way dew appears on grass. "It made me think of you."
The pages smelled of old paper and something sweeter underneath. Vanilla, maybe. Or decay. The two can smell identical.
"You shouldn't have," I said.
"I know," he said. "But I wanted to."

He wanted to. That was the phrase that got under my skin and stayed. Not *I thought you'd like it* or *it reminded me of you* — but *I wanted to.* Like the wanting was enough. Like his desire to give me things was a force of nature, separate from anything I'd done to earn it.
And God help me, I felt earned.
Thursday, the portrait.

He'd painted me from memory. Not a photograph — memory. And it was beautiful. Not flattering. Beautiful the way a wound is beautiful when it's healing clean. He'd caught something in the set of my jaw that I didn't think anyone could see — the stubbornness, the way I hold my ground even when I'm wrong. Especially when I'm wrong. My eyes in the painting were darker than mine really are, but the expression was perfect. Guarded. Hopeful underneath.
I touched the edge of the canvas. The paint was dry. It had been dry for a while.
"When did you do this?" I asked.
"Last week," he said. "While you were working."
"But — how? You weren't here last week."
He tilted his head. Patient. Always patient. "I was here Tuesday. You were reorganizing the herb cabinet. I sat in the corner and sketched."

I remembered Tuesday. I remembered the herb cabinet. I did not remember Julian. But the painting was real, and the light in it was the light from my shop window at two in the afternoon, and the angle was from the corner chair near the door.
"I guess I was focused," I said.
"You were," he agreed. That smile. It never broke. That should have been the warning — not the smile itself, but its permanence. Real smiles rest. His was a fixture.

I should have noticed. I should have felt the wrongness like a splinter under skin. Instead I felt chosen. I felt seen. I felt like the kind of woman a man paints from memory, the kind whose tea he knows without asking, the kind he finds impossible books for.
The kind who's different.
That word — *different* — it lodged in me like a fishhook. I am different from other women. I am the Aurelia. The last one. The one who breaks the pattern or becomes it.
What I didn't understand yet: believing you're different is the pattern.
Friday. The cliff café at four.

I know he said four. I know he said Friday. I wrote it in my phone — *Cliff café 4pm Friday* — and I remember writing it, the way the words looked in the text field, the way I hit send. I remember his reply: *Thursday. 4 sharp.* I remember the small flutter of correction — I'd gotten the day wrong and he'd fixed it. *Thursday. 4 sharp.* I remember reading it twice because I was embarrassed.
I arrived at the cliff café at 3:50 on Thursday. I ordered a coffee. I watched the door.
4:00. No Julian.
4:15. I checked my phone. His message was there. I reread it.
*This week. 4 works.*
This week. Not Thursday. This week.

My stomach dropped. It was a physical sensation — like stepping off a curb you didn't see, that lurch of the body before the mind catches up. I stared at the text. The words were right there. Black and white. *This week. 4 works.*
But that wasn't what it said this morning.

I know what I read. I KNOW what I read. He said Thursday. I remember the flutter of embarrassment when I'd gotten the day wrong, when he corrected me. I remember the specificity of it — Thursday, 4 sharp. The word *sharp* was there. I'm sure of it. I'm almost sure of it.
At 5:00, I texted him: *I'm at the café. Were we meeting today?*
His reply came at 5:45. *I said this week, not today. Are you okay?*

I reread the text chain from the beginning. Every message from him said *this week.* Every single one. The syntax was consistent. The phrasing was casual. No mention of Thursday. No *4 sharp.* Just the vague openness of *this week, 4 works.*
The letters sat on my screen and they looked... right. They looked like they had always been there.
But they hadn't. Had they?
I pressed my thumb against the screen like I could feel the ghost of other words underneath. The glass was cool and smooth. Nothing. No trace of the Thursday I remembered. No echo of *sharp.*
I sat at the cliff café for another forty minutes, watching the sea flatten into grey. The coffee went cold. I went home.
I mentioned it on Saturday. Casual. Light. The way you mention a thing when it's eating you but you don't want it to show.
"I could have sworn you said Thursday," I said. I was laughing a little. I wanted it to be funny. I wanted to be the kind of woman who laughs at her own bad memory.
He looked at me.

That look. I will never forget that look. Patient. Concerned. The way you look at someone who's said something deeply sad and doesn't know it yet. His eyes softened. His brow creased, just slightly. The portrait-stare, but reversed — instead of seeing me, he was showing me what I looked like through his eyes. And what I looked like was someone unwell.
"I would never forget our plans," he said. Gently. So gently. "Are you sleeping okay? You seem stressed."

The concern was real. That was the worst part. Or it felt real, which might be the same thing, which might be exactly the problem, which might be — I couldn't think about it. The concern was warm and it wrapped around me like a blanket and I wanted to sink into it.
"I'm fine," I said. "Just tired, I guess."
"You work too hard," he said. He reached across the table and took my hand. His thumb traced a small circle on my wrist. "Let me take care of you."
If I pressed — *But you DID say Thursday, I KNOW you did* — I would be paranoid. Difficult. One of those women. The kind who picks fights over nothing. The kind who can't let things go.
If I dropped it — and I dropped it — I doubted my own memory.
Either way, he won. Either way, the ground shifted an inch and I was standing on something less solid than before.
His thumb kept circling my wrist. I let it.
A week passed. Then two.

I hadn't been to the forest. The thought of it felt distant, like something I'd planned for another life. Leo's trail maps sat on the shop counter where I'd left them, curled at the edges, dust settling in the creases of the topographic lines. I kept meaning to fold them. I kept not doing it.

I hadn't visited Nadine. The drive seemed impossible — too far, too much, too tiring. Everything was too tiring now. My limbs were heavy and my sleep was wrong: deep and dreamless, like I was falling into a well and no one was pulling me up.

When Nadine called, I let it ring. Once. Twice. Three times. I watched her name on the screen and felt nothing, which was worse than feeling guilty. Guilt would have meant I knew something was wrong. Guilt would have meant some part of me was still listening.

Julian filled every gap. Morning coffee. Afternoon visits. Evening walks along the harbor. He was always there, and when he wasn't, his texts were — checking in, making plans, sending fragments of poetry he thought I'd like. He became my entire social world without me noticing the borders closing. It was like watching a room fill with water: you don't notice you're drowning until you try to breathe and can't.
I didn't try to breathe. I was comfortable. That was the horror of it. I was comfortable in the flood.

The shop suffered. I opened late. I closed early. I mixed up an order — valerian instead of vervain — and didn't notice until the customer came back. The lavender sachets from the attic hung in the doorway and their smell had changed, or I'd changed, or something. The scent was thicker now. Cloying. It caught in my throat like a word I couldn't say.
The fourth time Nadine called, I answered.

I don't know why. Maybe it was the lavender. Maybe it was the dust on Leo's maps. Maybe some part of me — the part that was still Aurelia, the part that hadn't been edited yet — reached for the phone before the tired part could stop it.
"Come home," she said.

No hello. No preamble. Just those two words, and her voice was so thin. Paper-thin. The kind of voice that comes from a body that has been hollowed out and is running on nothing but the echo of what it used to be.
"Grandma, I'm fine—"
"Please."

That word. *Please.* Not control. Not guilt. Not the manipulative softness of someone steering you. This was different. This was a woman who had lost herself in this exact pattern — the gifts, the attention, the slow replacement of everything familiar with one face, one voice, one certainty — and she was watching it happen again. To her granddaughter. And she was terrified.

I could hear it. The tremor underneath. The fear that had nothing to do with control and everything to do with love — love that was useless, love that couldn't reach across the distance, love that was watching a car crash in slow motion from the wrong side of the windshield.

But I was already too deep. Too tired. Too filled up with Julian's certainty to have room for Nadine's fear. Her terror felt like interference. Like she didn't understand. Like she was trying to ruin something good because she couldn't have it herself.
"I'm fine, Grandma," I said.
The oldest lie the curse ever told.
She was quiet for a long time. Then: "You sound like me."
She hung up.

I stood in the shop doorway with the phone against my chest, and the lavender smell was so strong I could taste it — floral and wrong and ancient, like something preserved in amber that should have been allowed to rot.
That night, I opened my journal.

I've kept one since I was twelve. Nadine gave me the habit. *Write it down,* she told me. *So you have proof.* Proof of what, I never asked. Proof that I existed. Proof that my memories belonged to me.
I wrote:

*He never said Thursday. Or did he? I can't remember. The words look like they were always "this week." They look right. They look like they've always been there, in that order, in that casual tone. But I remember a different word. I remember "sharp." I remember the specific weight of a day-of-the-week, the way Thursday feels different from "this week" — heavier, more real.*
*I can't remember.*
*I'm tired. I'm always tired now. It's in my bones, in the space between thoughts, in the pause before I speak. Everything takes longer than it should. Everything is fog.*
*And the lavender from the attic smells like a warning I can't translate.*

I stared at what I'd written. The handwriting was mine. The words were mine. But they read like someone else's confession — a woman losing her way in a place she used to know, a woman stepping off the path by millimeters and calling it walking.
I closed the journal. I turned off the light. I lay in the dark and listened to my own breathing and tried to remember Thursday.
The word wouldn't come clean. It kept smearing.

Somewhere in the house, the lavender thickened. Or maybe it was always that thick. Maybe I was only just noticing. Maybe the warning had been there all along and I'd been too busy feeling chosen to smell it.
The curse doesn't need me to love him.
It just needs me to doubt myself.
